sourceRefs.fallbackText: The Python runtime does not enforce function and variable type annotations. They can be used by third party tools such as type checkers , IDEs, linters, etc.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The Python runtime does not enforce function and variable type annotations. They can be used by third party tools such as type checkers , IDEs, linters, etc.

highSignalBlocks.text: The Python runtime does not enforce function and variable type annotations. They can be used by third party tools such as type checkers , IDEs, linters, etc.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This module provides runtime support for type hints.
sourceRefs.textQuote: This module provides runtime support for type hints.

highSignalBlocks.text: This module provides runtime support for type hints.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Consider the function below:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Consider the function below:

highSignalBlocks.text: Consider the function below:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: def surface_area_of_cube ( edge_length : float ) -> str : return f "The surface area of the cube is { 6 * edge_length ** 2 } ."
sourceRefs.textQuote: def surface_area_of_cube ( edge_length : float ) -> str : return f "The surface area of the cube is { 6 * edge_length ** 2 } ."

highSignalBlocks.text: def surface_area_of_cube ( edge_length : float ) -> str : return f "The surface area of the cube is { 6 * edge_length ** 2 } ."

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The function surface_area_of_cube takes an argument expected to be an instance of float , as indicated by the type hint edge_length: float . The function is expected to return an instance of str , as indicated by the -> str hint.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The function surface_area_of_cube takes an argument expected to be an instance of float , as indicated by the type hint edge_length: float . The function is expected to return an instance of str , as indicated by the ->

highSignalBlocks.text: The function surface_area_of_cube takes an argument expected to be an instance of float , as indicated by the type hint edge_length: float . The function is expected to return an instance of str , as indicated by the -> str hint.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: While type hints can be simple classes like float or str , they can also be more complex. The typing module provides a vocabulary of more advanced type hints.
sourceRefs.textQuote: While type hints can be simple classes like float or str , they can also be more complex. The typing module provides a vocabulary of more advanced type hints.

highSignalBlocks.text: While type hints can be simple classes like float or str , they can also be more complex. The typing module provides a vocabulary of more advanced type hints.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: New features are frequently added to the typing module. The typing_extensions package provides backports of these new features to older versions of Python.
sourceRefs.textQuote: New features are frequently added to the typing module. The typing_extensions package provides backports of these new features to older versions of Python.

highSignalBlocks.text: New features are frequently added to the typing module. The typing_extensions package provides backports of these new features to older versions of Python.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A quick overview of type hints (hosted at the mypy docs)
sourceRefs.textQuote: A quick overview of type hints (hosted at the mypy docs)

highSignalBlocks.text: A quick overview of type hints (hosted at the mypy docs)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The Python typing system is standardised via PEPs, so this reference should broadly apply to most Python type checkers. (Some parts may still be specific to mypy.)
sourceRefs.textQuote: The Python typing system is standardised via PEPs, so this reference should broadly apply to most Python type checkers. (Some parts may still be specific to mypy.)

highSignalBlocks.text: The Python typing system is standardised via PEPs, so this reference should broadly apply to most Python type checkers. (Some parts may still be specific to mypy.)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The canonical, up-to-date specification of the Python type system can be found at Specification for the Python type system .
headingPath: Specification for the Python Type System ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: The canonical, up-to-date specification of the Python type system can be found at Specification for the Python type system .

highSignalBlocks.text: The canonical, up-to-date specification of the Python type system can be found at Specification for the Python type system .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A type alias is defined using the type statement, which creates an instance of TypeAliasType . In this example, Vector and list[float] will be treated equivalently by static type checkers:
sourceRefs.textQuote: A type alias is defined using the type statement, which creates an instance of TypeAliasType . In this example, Vector and list[float] will be treated equivalently by static type checkers:

highSignalBlocks.text: A type alias is defined using the type statement, which creates an instance of TypeAliasType . In this example, Vector and list[float] will be treated equivalently by static type checkers:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: type Vector = list [ float ] def scale ( scalar : float , vector : Vector ) -> Vector : return [ scalar * num for num in vector ] # passes type checking; a list of floats qualifies as a Vector. new_vector = scale ( 2.0 , [ 1.0 , - 4.2 , 5.4 ])
sourceRefs.textQuote: type Vector = list [ float ] def scale ( scalar : float , vector : Vector ) -> Vector : return [ scalar * num for num in vector ] # passes type checking; a list of floats qualifies as a Vector. new_vector = scale ( 2.0 ,

highSignalBlocks.text: type Vector = list [ float ] def scale ( scalar : float , vector : Vector ) -> Vector : return [ scalar * num for num in vector ] # passes type checking; a list of floats qualifies as a Vector. new_vector = scale ( 2.0 , [ 1.0 , - 4.2 , 5.4 ])

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Type aliases are useful for simplifying complex type signatures. For example:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Type aliases are useful for simplifying complex type signatures. For example:

highSignalBlocks.text: Type aliases are useful for simplifying complex type signatures. For example:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: from collections.abc import Sequence type ConnectionOptions = dict [ str , str ] type Address = tuple [ str , int ] type Server = tuple [ Address , ConnectionOptions ] def broadcast_message ( message : str , servers : Sequence [ Server ]) -> None : ... # The static type checker will treat the previous type signature as # being exactly equivalent to this one.
sourceRefs.textQuote: from collections.abc import Sequence type ConnectionOptions = dict [ str , str ] type Address = tuple [ str , int ] type Server = tuple [ Address , ConnectionOptions ] def broadcast_message ( message : str , servers : Se

highSignalBlocks.text: from collections.abc import Sequence type ConnectionOptions = dict [ str , str ] type Address = tuple [ str , int ] type Server = tuple [ Address , ConnectionOptions ] def broadcast_message ( message : str , servers : Sequence [ Server ]) -> None : ... # The static type checker will treat the previous type signature as # being exactly equivalent to this one. def broadcast_message ( message : str , servers : Sequence [ tuple [ tuple [ str , int ], dict [ str , str ]]] ) -> None : ...

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The type statement is new in Python 3.12. For backwards compatibility, type aliases can also be created through simple assignment:
sourceRefs.textQuote: The type statement is new in Python 3.12. For backwards compatibility, type aliases can also be created through simple assignment:

highSignalBlocks.text: The type statement is new in Python 3.12. For backwards compatibility, type aliases can also be created through simple assignment:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Vector = list [ float ]
sourceRefs.textQuote: Vector = list [ float ]

highSignalBlocks.text: Vector = list [ float ]

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Or marked with TypeAlias to make it explicit that this is a type alias, not a normal variable assignment:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Or marked with TypeAlias to make it explicit that this is a type alias, not a normal variable assignment:

highSignalBlocks.text: Or marked with TypeAlias to make it explicit that this is a type alias, not a normal variable assignment:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: from typing import TypeAlias Vector : TypeAlias = list [ float ]
sourceRefs.textQuote: from typing import TypeAlias Vector : TypeAlias = list [ float ]

highSignalBlocks.text: from typing import TypeAlias Vector : TypeAlias = list [ float ]

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Use the NewType helper to create distinct types:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Use the NewType helper to create distinct types:

highSignalBlocks.text: Use the NewType helper to create distinct types:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: from typing import NewType UserId = NewType ( 'UserId' , int ) some_id = UserId ( 524313 )
sourceRefs.textQuote: from typing import NewType UserId = NewType ( 'UserId' , int ) some_id = UserId ( 524313 )

highSignalBlocks.text: from typing import NewType UserId = NewType ( 'UserId' , int ) some_id = UserId ( 524313 )

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The static type checker will treat the new type as if it were a subclass of the original type. This is useful in helping catch logical errors:
sourceRefs.textQuote: The static type checker will treat the new type as if it were a subclass of the original type. This is useful in helping catch logical errors:

highSignalBlocks.text: The static type checker will treat the new type as if it were a subclass of the original type. This is useful in helping catch logical errors:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: def get_user_name ( user_id : UserId ) -> str : ... # passes type checking user_a = get_user_name ( UserId ( 42351 )) # fails type checking; an int is not a UserId user_b = get_user_name ( - 1 )
sourceRefs.textQuote: def get_user_name ( user_id : UserId ) -> str : ... # passes type checking user_a = get_user_name ( UserId ( 42351 )) # fails type checking; an int is not a UserId user_b = get_user_name ( - 1 )

highSignalBlocks.text: def get_user_name ( user_id : UserId ) -> str : ... # passes type checking user_a = get_user_name ( UserId ( 42351 )) # fails type checking; an int is not a UserId user_b = get_user_name ( - 1 )

sourceRefs.fallbackText: You may still perform all int operations on a variable of type UserId , but the result will always be of type int . This lets you pass in a UserId wherever an int might be expected, but will prevent you from accidentally creating a UserId in an invalid way:
sourceRefs.textQuote: You may still perform all int operations on a variable of type UserId , but the result will always be of type int . This lets you pass in a UserId wherever an int might be expected, but will prevent you from accidentally

highSignalBlocks.text: You may still perform all int operations on a variable of type UserId , but the result will always be of type int . This lets you pass in a UserId wherever an int might be expected, but will prevent you from accidentally creating a UserId in an invalid way:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: # 'output' is of type 'int', not 'UserId' output = UserId ( 23413 ) + UserId ( 54341 )
sourceRefs.textQuote: # 'output' is of type 'int', not 'UserId' output = UserId ( 23413 ) + UserId ( 54341 )

highSignalBlocks.text: # 'output' is of type 'int', not 'UserId' output = UserId ( 23413 ) + UserId ( 54341 )

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Note that these checks are enforced only by the static type checker. At runtime, the statement Derived = NewType('Derived', Base) will make Derived a callable that immediately returns whatever parameter you pass it. That means the expression Derived(some_value) does not create a new class or introduce much overhead beyond that of a regular function call.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Note that these checks are enforced only by the static type checker. At runtime, the statement Derived = NewType('Derived', Base) will make Derived a callable that immediately returns whatever parameter you pass it. That

highSignalBlocks.text: Note that these checks are enforced only by the static type checker. At runtime, the statement Derived = NewType('Derived', Base) will make Derived a callable that immediately returns whatever parameter you pass it. That means the expression Derived(some_value) does not create a new class or introduce much overhead beyond that of a regular function call.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: More precisely, the expression some_value is Derived(some_value) is always true at runtime.
sourceRefs.textQuote: More precisely, the expression some_value is Derived(some_value) is always true at runtime.

highSignalBlocks.text: More precisely, the expression some_value is Derived(some_value) is always true at runtime.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: It is invalid to create a subtype of Derived :
sourceRefs.textQuote: It is invalid to create a subtype of Derived :

highSignalBlocks.text: It is invalid to create a subtype of Derived :

sourceRefs.fallbackText: from typing import NewType UserId = NewType ( 'UserId' , int ) # Fails at runtime and does not pass type checking class AdminUserId ( UserId ): pass
sourceRefs.textQuote: from typing import NewType UserId = NewType ( 'UserId' , int ) # Fails at runtime and does not pass type checking class AdminUserId ( UserId ): pass

highSignalBlocks.text: from typing import NewType UserId = NewType ( 'UserId' , int ) # Fails at runtime and does not pass type checking class AdminUserId ( UserId ): pass

sourceRefs.fallbackText: However, it is possible to create a NewType based on a ‘derived’ NewType :
sourceRefs.textQuote: However, it is possible to create a NewType based on a ‘derived’ NewType :

highSignalBlocks.text: However, it is possible to create a NewType based on a ‘derived’ NewType :

sourceRefs.fallbackText: from typing import NewType UserId = NewType ( 'UserId' , int ) ProUserId = NewType ( 'ProUserId' , UserId )
sourceRefs.textQuote: from typing import NewType UserId = NewType ( 'UserId' , int ) ProUserId = NewType ( 'ProUserId' , UserId )

highSignalBlocks.text: from typing import NewType UserId = NewType ( 'UserId' , int ) ProUserId = NewType ( 'ProUserId' , UserId )

sourceRefs.fallbackText: and typechecking for ProUserId will work as expected.
sourceRefs.textQuote: and typechecking for ProUserId will work as expected.

highSignalBlocks.text: and typechecking for ProUserId will work as expected.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: See PEP 484 for more details.
sourceRefs.textQuote: See PEP 484 for more details.

highSignalBlocks.text: See PEP 484 for more details.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Recall that the use of a type alias declares two types to be equivalent to one another. Doing type Alias = Original will make the static type checker treat Alias as being exactly equivalent to Original in all cases. This is useful when you want to simplify complex type signatures.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Recall that the use of a type alias declares two types to be equivalent to one another. Doing type Alias = Original will make the static type checker treat Alias as being exactly equivalent to Original in all cases. This

highSignalBlocks.text: Recall that the use of a type alias declares two types to be equivalent to one another. Doing type Alias = Original will make the static type checker treat Alias as being exactly equivalent to Original in all cases. This is useful when you want to simplify complex type signatures.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: In contrast, NewType declares one type to be a subtype of another. Doing Derived = NewType('Derived', Original) will make the static type checker treat Derived as a subclass of Original , which means a value of type Original cannot be used in places where a value of type Derived is expected. This is useful when you want to prevent logic errors with minimal r
sourceRefs.textQuote: In contrast, NewType declares one type to be a subtype of another. Doing Derived = NewType('Derived', Original) will make the static type checker treat Derived as a subclass of Original , which means a value of type Orig

highSignalBlocks.text: In contrast, NewType declares one type to be a subtype of another. Doing Derived = NewType('Derived', Original) will make the static type checker treat Derived as a subclass of Original , which means a value of type Original cannot be used in places where a value of type Derived is expected. This is useful when you want to prevent logic errors with minimal runtime cost.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Added in version 3.5.2.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Added in version 3.5.2.

highSignalBlocks.text: Added in version 3.5.2.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Changed in version 3.10: NewType is now a class rather than a function. As a result, there is some additional runtime cost when calling NewType over a regular function.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Changed in version 3.10: NewType is now a class rather than a function. As a result, there is some additional runtime cost when calling NewType over a regular function.

highSignalBlocks.text: Changed in version 3.10: NewType is now a class rather than a function. As a result, there is some additional runtime cost when calling NewType over a regular function.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Changed in version 3.11: The performance of calling NewType has been restored to its level in Python 3.9.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Changed in version 3.11: The performance of calling NewType has been restored to its level in Python 3.9.

highSignalBlocks.text: Changed in version 3.11: The performance of calling NewType has been restored to its level in Python 3.9.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Functions – or other callable objects – can be annotated using collections.abc.Callable or deprecated typing.Callable . Callable[[int], str] signifies a function that takes a single parameter of type int and returns a str .
sourceRefs.textQuote: Functions – or other callable objects – can be annotated using collections.abc.Callable or deprecated typing.Callable . Callable[[int], str] signifies a function that takes a single parameter of type int and returns a st

highSignalBlocks.text: Functions – or other callable objects – can be annotated using collections.abc.Callable or deprecated typing.Callable . Callable[[int], str] signifies a function that takes a single parameter of type int and returns a str .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: from collections.abc import Callable , Awaitable def feeder ( get_next_item : Callable [[], str ]) -> None : ... # Body def async_query ( on_success : Callable [[ int ], None ], on_error : Callable [[ int , Exception ], None ]) -> None : ... # Body async def on_update ( value : str ) -> None : ... # Body callback : Callable [[ str ], Awaitable [ None ]] = on
sourceRefs.textQuote: from collections.abc import Callable , Awaitable def feeder ( get_next_item : Callable [[], str ]) -> None : ... # Body def async_query ( on_success : Callable [[ int ], None ], on_error : Callable [[ int , Exception ],

highSignalBlocks.text: from collections.abc import Callable , Awaitable def feeder ( get_next_item : Callable [[], str ]) -> None : ... # Body def async_query ( on_success : Callable [[ int ], None ], on_error : Callable [[ int , Exception ], None ]) -> None : ... # Body async def on_update ( value : str ) -> None : ... # Body callback : Callable [[ str ], Awaitable [ None ]] = on_update

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The subscription syntax must always be used with exactly two values: the argument list and the return type. The argument list must be a list of types, a ParamSpec , Concatenate , or an ellipsis ( ... ). The return type must be a single type.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The subscription syntax must always be used with exactly two values: the argument list and the return type. The argument list must be a list of types, a ParamSpec , Concatenate , or an ellipsis ( ... ). The return type m

highSignalBlocks.text: The subscription syntax must always be used with exactly two values: the argument list and the return type. The argument list must be a list of types, a ParamSpec , Concatenate , or an ellipsis ( ... ). The return type must be a single type.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If a literal ellipsis ... is given as the argument list, it indicates that a callable with any arbitrary parameter list would be acceptable:
sourceRefs.textQuote: If a literal ellipsis ... is given as the argument list, it indicates that a callable with any arbitrary parameter list would be acceptable:

highSignalBlocks.text: If a literal ellipsis ... is given as the argument list, it indicates that a callable with any arbitrary parameter list would be acceptable:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: def concat ( x : str , y : str ) -> str : return x + y x : Callable [ ... , str ] x = str # OK x = concat # Also OK
sourceRefs.textQuote: def concat ( x : str , y : str ) -> str : return x + y x : Callable [ ... , str ] x = str # OK x = concat # Also OK

highSignalBlocks.text: def concat ( x : str , y : str ) -> str : return x + y x : Callable [ ... , str ] x = str # OK x = concat # Also OK

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Callable cannot express complex signatures such as functions that take a variadic number of arguments, overloaded functions , or functions that have keyword-only parameters. However, these signatures can be expressed by defining a Protocol class with a __call__() method:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Callable cannot express complex signatures such as functions that take a variadic number of arguments, overloaded functions , or functions that have keyword-only parameters. However, these signatures can be expressed by

highSignalBlocks.text: Callable cannot express complex signatures such as functions that take a variadic number of arguments, overloaded functions , or functions that have keyword-only parameters. However, these signatures can be expressed by defining a Protocol class with a __call__() method:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: from collections.abc import Iterable from typing import Protocol class Combiner ( Protocol ): def __call__ ( self , * vals : bytes , maxlen : int | None = None ) -> list [ bytes ]: ... def batch_proc ( data : Iterable [ bytes ], cb_results : Combiner ) -> bytes : for item in data : ... def good_cb ( * vals : bytes , maxlen : int | None = None ) -> list [ byt
sourceRefs.textQuote: from collections.abc import Iterable from typing import Protocol class Combiner ( Protocol ): def __call__ ( self , * vals : bytes , maxlen : int | None = None ) -> list [ bytes ]: ... def batch_proc ( data : Iterable [

highSignalBlocks.text: from collections.abc import Iterable from typing import Protocol class Combiner ( Protocol ): def __call__ ( self , * vals : bytes , maxlen : int | None = None ) -> list [ bytes ]: ... def batch_proc ( data : Iterable [ bytes ], cb_results : Combiner ) -> bytes : for item in data : ... def good_cb ( * vals : bytes , maxlen : int | None = None ) -> list [ bytes ]: ... def bad_cb ( * vals : bytes , maxitems : int | None ) -> list [ bytes ]: ... batch_proc ([], good_cb ) # OK batch_proc ([], bad_cb ) # Error! Argument 2 has incompatible type because of # different name and kind in the callback

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Callables which take other callables as arguments may indicate that their parameter types are dependent on each other using ParamSpec . Additionally, if that callable adds or removes arguments from other callables, the Concatenate operator may be used. They take the form Callable[ParamSpecVariable, ReturnType] and Callable[Concatenate[Arg1Type, Arg2Type, ...
sourceRefs.textQuote: Callables which take other callables as arguments may indicate that their parameter types are dependent on each other using ParamSpec . Additionally, if that callable adds or removes arguments from other callables, the C

highSignalBlocks.text: Callables which take other callables as arguments may indicate that their parameter types are dependent on each other using ParamSpec . Additionally, if that callable adds or removes arguments from other callables, the Concatenate operator may be used. They take the form Callable[ParamSpecVariable, ReturnType] and Callable[Concatenate[Arg1Type, Arg2Type, ..., ParamSpecVariable], ReturnType] respectively.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Changed in version 3.10: Callable now supports ParamSpec and Concatenate . See PEP 612 for more details.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Changed in version 3.10: Callable now supports ParamSpec and Concatenate . See PEP 612 for more details.

highSignalBlocks.text: Changed in version 3.10: Callable now supports ParamSpec and Concatenate . See PEP 612 for more details.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The documentation for ParamSpec and Concatenate provides examples of usage in Callable .
sourceRefs.textQuote: The documentation for ParamSpec and Concatenate provides examples of usage in Callable .

highSignalBlocks.text: The documentation for ParamSpec and Concatenate provides examples of usage in Callable .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Since type information about objects kept in containers cannot be statically inferred in a generic way, many container classes in the standard library support subscription to denote the expected types of container elements.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Since type information about objects kept in containers cannot be statically inferred in a generic way, many container classes in the standard library support subscription to denote the expected types of container elemen

sourceRefs.fallbackText: from collections.abc import Mapping # Type checker will infer that all elements in ``x`` are meant to be ints x : list [ int ] = [] # Type checker error: ``list`` only accepts a single type argument: y : list [ int , str ] = [ 1 , 'foo' ] # Type checker will infer that all keys in ``z`` are meant to be strings, # and that all values in ``z`` are meant to be
sourceRefs.textQuote: from collections.abc import Mapping # Type checker will infer that all elements in ``x`` are meant to be ints x : list [ int ] = [] # Type checker error: ``list`` only accepts a single type argument: y : list [ int , str

highSignalBlocks.text: from collections.abc import Mapping # Type checker will infer that all elements in ``x`` are meant to be ints x : list [ int ] = [] # Type checker error: ``list`` only accepts a single type argument: y : list [ int , str ] = [ 1 , 'foo' ] # Type checker will infer that all keys in ``z`` are meant to be strings, # and that all values in ``z`` are meant to be either strings or ints z : Mapping [ str , str | int ] = {}

sourceRefs.fallbackText: list only accepts one type argument, so a type checker would emit an error on the y assignment above. Similarly, Mapping only accepts two type arguments: the first indicates the type of the keys, and the second indicates the type of the values.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Unlike most other Python containers, however, it is common in idiomatic Python code for tuples to have elements which are not all of the same type. For this reason, tuples are special-cased in Python’s typing system. tuple accepts any number of type arguments:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Unlike most other Python containers, however, it is common in idiomatic Python code for tuples to have elements which are not all of the same type. For this reason, tuples are special-cased in Python’s typing system. tup

sourceRefs.fallbackText: # OK: ``x`` is assigned to a tuple of length 1 where the sole element is an int x : tuple [ int ] = ( 5 ,) # OK: ``y`` is assigned to a tuple of length 2; # element 1 is an int, element 2 is a str y : tuple [ int , str ] = ( 5 , "foo" ) # Error: the type annotation indicates a tuple of length 1, # but ``z`` has been assigned to a tuple of length 3 z : tuple
sourceRefs.textQuote: # OK: ``x`` is assigned to a tuple of length 1 where the sole element is an int x : tuple [ int ] = ( 5 ,) # OK: ``y`` is assigned to a tuple of length 2; # element 1 is an int, element 2 is a str y : tuple [ int , str ]

highSignalBlocks.text: # OK: ``x`` is assigned to a tuple of length 1 where the sole element is an int x : tuple [ int ] = ( 5 ,) # OK: ``y`` is assigned to a tuple of length 2; # element 1 is an int, element 2 is a str y : tuple [ int , str ] = ( 5 , "foo" ) # Error: the type annotation indicates a tuple of length 1, # but ``z`` has been assigned to a tuple of length 3 z : tuple [ int ] = ( 1 , 2 , 3 )

sourceRefs.fallbackText: To denote a tuple which could be of any length, and in which all elements are of the same type T , use the literal ellipsis ... : tuple[T, ...] . To denote an empty tuple, use tuple[()] . Using plain tuple as an annotation is equivalent to using tuple[Any, ...] :

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A variable annotated with C may accept a value of type C . In contrast, a variable annotated with type[C] (or deprecated typing.Type[C] ) may accept values that are classes themselves – specifically, it will accept the class object of C . For example:
sourceRefs.textQuote: A variable annotated with C may accept a value of type C . In contrast, a variable annotated with type[C] (or deprecated typing.Type[C] ) may accept values that are classes themselves – specifically, it will accept the c

sourceRefs.fallbackText: a = 3 # Has type ``int`` b = int # Has type ``type[int]`` c = type ( a ) # Also has type ``type[int]``
sourceRefs.textQuote: a = 3 # Has type ``int`` b = int # Has type ``type[int]`` c = type ( a ) # Also has type ``type[int]``

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A generator can be annotated using the generic type Generator[YieldType, SendType, ReturnType] . For example:
sourceRefs.textQuote: A generator can be annotated using the generic type Generator[YieldType, SendType, ReturnType] . For example:

highSignalBlocks.text: A generator can be annotated using the generic type Generator[YieldType, SendType, ReturnType] . For example:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: def echo_round () -> Generator [ int , float , str ]: sent = yield 0 while sent >= 0 : sent = yield round ( sent ) return 'Done'
sourceRefs.textQuote: def echo_round () -> Generator [ int , float , str ]: sent = yield 0 while sent >= 0 : sent = yield round ( sent ) return 'Done'

highSignalBlocks.text: def echo_round () -> Generator [ int , float , str ]: sent = yield 0 while sent >= 0 : sent = yield round ( sent ) return 'Done'

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Note that unlike many other generic classes in the standard library, the SendType of Generator behaves contravariantly, not covariantly or invariantly.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Note that unlike many other generic classes in the standard library, the SendType of Generator behaves contravariantly, not covariantly or invariantly.

highSignalBlocks.text: Note that unlike many other generic classes in the standard library, the SendType of Generator behaves contravariantly, not covariantly or invariantly.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: from logging import Logger class LoggedVar [ T ]: def __init__ ( self , value : T , name : str , logger : Logger ) -> None : self . name = name self . logger = logger self . value = value def set ( self , new : T ) -> None : self . log ( 'Set ' + repr ( self . value )) self . value = new def get ( self ) -> T : self . log ( 'Get ' + repr ( self . value )) re
sourceRefs.textQuote: from logging import Logger class LoggedVar [ T ]: def __init__ ( self , value : T , name : str , logger : Logger ) -> None : self . name = name self . logger = logger self . value = value def set ( self , new : T ) -> No

highSignalBlocks.text: from logging import Logger class LoggedVar [ T ]: def __init__ ( self , value : T , name : str , logger : Logger ) -> None : self . name = name self . logger = logger self . value = value def set ( self , new : T ) -> None : self . log ( 'Set ' + repr ( self . value )) self . value = new def get ( self ) -> T : self . log ( 'Get ' + repr ( self . value )) return self . value def log ( self , message : str ) -> None : self . logger . info ( ' %s : %s ' , self . name , message )

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Generic classes implicitly inherit from Generic . For compatibility with Python 3.11 and lower, it is also possible to inherit explicitly from Generic to indicate a generic class:
